# Demonstration of differential coverage with testcallgraph

# Create a test that runs different programs
exec mkdir -p tests
exec sh -c 'printf "exec mcpdiff --help\\nexec mcp-spy -- trace.json\\n" > tests/test1.txt'
exec sh -c 'printf "exec mcpspy -- trace.json\\nexec mcp-test -- go test ./...\\nexec go test -cover ./pkg/...\\n" > tests/test2.txt'

# Simulate baseline coverage
exec mkdir -p cov/baseline
exec sh -c 'printf "github.com/tmc/mcp/cmd/mcpdiff\\t70.5%%\\ngithub.com/tmc/mcp/cmd/mcp-spy\\t65.0%%\\ngithub.com/tmc/mcp/core\\t80.0%%\\n" > cov/baseline/summary.txt'

# Simulate improved coverage after running test2
exec mkdir -p cov/after_test2
exec sh -c 'printf "github.com/tmc/mcp/cmd/mcpdiff\\t70.5%%\\ngithub.com/tmc/mcp/cmd/mcp-spy\\t68.0%%\\ngithub.com/tmc/mcp/cmd/mcp-test\\t55.0%%\\ngithub.com/tmc/mcp/core\\t82.5%%\\ngithub.com/tmc/mcp/pkg\\t45.0%%\\n" > cov/after_test2/summary.txt'

# Analyze which test contributed to coverage improvements
echo "=== Analyzing test contributions to coverage ==="

# Show test1 programs
exec testcallgraph -analyze mcpdiff tests/test1.txt
exec testcallgraph -analyze mcp-spy tests/test1.txt

# Show test2 programs and coverage impact
exec testcallgraph tests/test2.txt
stdout 'mcp-test'
stdout 'go.*test'

echo "=== Coverage impact analysis ==="
echo "test1.txt: Uses mcpdiff, mcp-spy (no coverage change)"
echo "test2.txt: Uses mcp-spy, mcp-test, go test"
echo "  - Improved mcp-spy coverage by 3%"
echo "  - Added new coverage for mcp-test (55%)"
echo "  - Added new coverage for pkg (45%)"
echo "  - Improved core coverage by 2.5%"

# This demonstrates how testcallgraph can help identify
# which tests are responsible for coverage improvements
